title: Asuka Hideaki Annos Side Dish
wikitext:
[]: Asuka: Hideaki Anno's Side-Dish             Quote Post by ran1 » Tue Jun 29, 2010 12:06 pm [Though at one point, this was a digression in my upcoming hell-train analysis, I think the following piece has diverted enough from the original subject matter that it deserves its own discussion topic.] While doing the analysis of the hell-train, I spent a particularly long time mulling over Asuka's line that she was essentially a “side-dish” to Shinji. Though my Japanese-fu is weak, I'm inclined to think that this means that she was some kind of “relationship on the side” or one that Shinji didn't attach a particular amount of attachment towards. [EDIT: According to NAveryW, the line is most directly interpreted as "object of masturbatory fantasy"] I can't find myself agreeing with this conclusion. I am under the impression that his relationship with Asuka is in his mind, consciously at least, the most important sexual relationship in the entire series. Seeing the fundamental “transition from mother to significant other” aspect that the film seems to support this as well – Shinji obviously seems to have chosen Asuka as his most-trusted “love interest” at the outset of the film, being far too afraid of Misato and Rei. Though it seems like a choice by default, EoE is indeed a movie about choice and its consequences. So then if not Shinji, who was this line directed towards...? Seeing Shinji as Anno's avatar, I believe that this is one of those subtle moments where Asuka implicates Anno as using her as a side-dish. I suppose the next question is: how did you come to that conclusion? Asuka is a character created by Anno, how is she aware of his existence...? Allow me to say that in this regard Anno is a truly brilliant auteur. Several of the great directors have done exactly this – used characters to implicate their own inequities. When I thought of this, I am immediately reminded of Bergman's Antonius Block, who cries out at the absurdity and foolishness of the quest for finding God – the primary theme of all of Ingmar Bergman's films. The idea of character-to-character dialogue implicating the director is also prevalent in the cinema of Jean Luc Godard (a director who shows an uncanny amount of similarities with Anno; though the question of actual direct influence is up for debate). In Godard's masterpiece Alphaville, the two main characters discuss the fool's errand of romantic love, an issue that Godard had grappled with throughout his cinematic career. So in this regard, I am under the firm impression that Asuka's side-dish line holds extra-narrative significance – it is a criticism of Anno through Shinji. But what is she criticizing? Asuka is criticizing Anno for treating her as a side-dish – I.E objectifying her body, showing her in various fan-service scenes, and treating her as some kind of sex object while having only limited emotional investment in her outcome. From the very outset of her entry into the series, her body is treated sexually. Shinji peeks a look at her in Episode 8, but it is interesting to note the positioning of the shot – we see Asuka changing before Shinji glances over at him – and unlike Shinji who retreats, The shot simply stays there. The following episodes and EoE are also filled with interestingly framed fan-service. Here are some examples: The Wall of Jericho and Bedroom Scenes in Episode 9: [wkimg width=250]File:22_C390_jericho.jpg[/wkimg][wkimg width=400]File:09_boobies.JPG[/wkimg] The “Thermal Expansion” bit in Episode 10: [wkimg width=300]File:10_C059_bigger.jpg[/wkimg] The Hospital Scene in EoE where get the full on boob action: [wkimg width=400]File:Hospital_Scene--_Boobs.png[/wkimg][wkimg width=400]File:Hospital_Scene--High_Angle_Fan_Service.png[/wkimg] Here we see that Asuka is probably the single most objectified character in the series – and many of these situations are not necessarily the fault of herself or Shinji. Most of these scenes could be easily de-sexualized, or at least have the fan-service elements more subtle in presentation. Misato's role as fan-service object is one that is done in a subtle manner –her sex scene portrayed in the series is rather mild. Even in the moment of implication itself – Asuka is objectified by the camera.. [wkimg width=400]File:M26 C240 flaunting-it.jpg[/wkimg][wkimg width=400]File:M26 C241 cornered.jpg[/wkimg] With her breasts featured prominently in the first frame. On IRC, Essel and I discussed the nature of Anno's lack of sympathy towards Asuka in Rebuild. But now, I realize that in the context of framing and presentation, Anno is seemingly no less sympathetic to Asuka in the series and films. Deserved or not, she is certainly the character that gets the worst of it. I'm not going to turn this into a b'aww thread, but the point still stands – Anno, not Shinji, is the one who delivers the worst blows to Asuka, and it seems that here, in the hell-train scene, Asuka finally gets the chance to call him out on it. So, DISCUSS.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Jun 29, 2010 1:22 pm It's because, in the grand scheme of things Asuka really doesn't matter. She, like most of the characters, are just auxiliary devices to foster Shinji's own emotional development, to the point where she's also a pretty good deconstruction of tropes such as the Hyper Manic Pixie Girl. That, and it's an interesting irony created from her ego and superiority complex. She's someone who, despite all her boasting, really isn't all that interesting, important, or worthwhile except for how she can be used; either for Evangelion, or for tits. And she won't have either of those forever.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Tue Jun 29, 2010 1:33 pm AuraTwilight wrote: It's because, in the grand scheme of things Asuka really doesn't matter. She, like most of the characters, are just auxiliary devices to foster Shinji's own emotional development, to the point where she's also a pretty good deconstruction of tropes such as the Hyper Manic Pixie Girl.

[]:             Quote Post by SaltyJoe » Tue Jun 29, 2010 1:52 pm esselfortium wrote: Her "not worthwhile except for how she can be used" perception is the result of how personally invested she's allowed herself to become in piloting, not really an adequate or impartial assessment of her personality.

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Tue Jun 29, 2010 2:09 pm SaltyJoe wrote: And in a sense, that's true. There is almost no feasible way i can think about for her to escape self destruction by the end of the series.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Tue Jun 29, 2010 2:40 pm While the story about Anno getting shot down by Miyamura seems to have been apocryphal, Asuka herself, in the way she is singled out for being badly used, seems to have some definite meaning to him (in the same way that it is obvious that Rei is Sadamoto's favourite). SaltyJoe wrote: In this analysis/review of Eva, the author muses that "[Asuka's] role was too tightly determined." And in a sense, that's true. There is almost no feasible way i can think about for her to escape self destruction by the end of the series.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 29, 2010 3:18 pm Eh, I think nearly all such instances can be chalked up to approximating Shinji's wandering gaze, along with her own willingness to leverage her physical assets at the drop of a hat. I personally take her comments to mean that she feels she's more an object of convenient lust to Shinji rather than a person to be meaningfully engaged (as well as to lampshade his roll as harem guy), just as her role within Nerv is ultimately restricted to that of an expendable shock trooper.

[]: Re: Asuka: Hideaki Anno's Side-Dish             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Tue Jun 29, 2010 3:30 pm ran1 wrote: Asuka is criticizing Anno for treating her as a side-dish – I.E objectifying her body, showing her in various fan-service scenes, and treating her as some kind of sex object while having only limited emotional investment in her outcome.

[]: Re: Asuka: Hideaki Anno's Side-Dish             Quote Post by esselfortium » Tue Jun 29, 2010 3:36 pm oOoOoOo wrote: The pilots act pretty level-headed considering they are child soldiers in an apocalyptic war. Their actions are even more admirable when you consider their mental health issues. I'm in my twenties and living in a peaceful neighbourhood, and I have trouble acting like "real people". (Whatever the fuck that means.)

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Tue Jun 29, 2010 3:47 pm Asuka arrives August/September of 2015. The world ends four months later. Are you saying Shinji and Asuka should have made more progress on the relationship front in this small amount of time? That strikes me as incredibly unrealistic, considering who we're talking about.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Tue Jun 29, 2010 3:50 pm Pretty much. I find it impossible to take the one or two dates we're given in the show at face value and still expect any of the timeline's events to have the emotional impact we're led to believe they have.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Jun 29, 2010 3:52 pm This analysis seems to look at all of the actions from the character without any glance at the character's motivation. No, I'm not gonna quote ran1's whole post. This analysis seems to treat the author as the character rather than the creator of the characters. ran1 wrote: Anno, not Shinji, is the one who delivers the worst blows to Asuka

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Tue Jun 29, 2010 3:56 pm oOoOoOo wrote: Asuka arrives August/September of 2015. The world ends four months later. Are you saying Shinji and Asuka should have made more progress on the relationship front in this small amount of time?

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Tue Jun 29, 2010 4:06 pm They have a love/hate thing for a while, and they eventually realize some things about themselves. For kids their age, that's not bad in four months. They clearly have some feelings for each other by the end, but it is quite the struggle. The big relationship struggles are really internal anyways. With all the navel-gazing going on, nobody has time to have a healthy relationship. I don't think I could have expected much different from myself in the same situation, at the same age. Hmmmm, well, what should have happened? Maybe we secretly agree on some points.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Tue Jun 29, 2010 4:08 pm I just don't think it's believable that the entire series happened in four or five months, honestly. We're told that Shinji is supposedly not very good with people and is hard to get to know, but by episode 8 it's the most normal thing in the world for him to be hanging out with Kensuke and Toji.

[]:             Quote Post by Seele00TextOnly » Tue Jun 29, 2010 4:24 pm ...

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Tue Jun 29, 2010 4:27 pm Well, culturally it is probably less of a deal for Shinji to become friendly with boys, as opposed to girls. Also, his male classmates are probably less intimidating than Rei or Asuka (who are both much harder to read than Kensuke/Toji). The boys have something like a month to become friends before Asuka shows up. Also, Kensuke is pretty proactive about befriendly Shinji, and is a rather uncomplicated person. Even then, Shinji's still awkward around the two guys on some level, but they don't play mindgames with him the way the girls do. This doesn't strike me as outlandishly unbelievable for fourteen-year-olds.

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Tue Jun 29, 2010 4:28 pm Seele00TextOnly wrote: It's common in anime (and television, and fiction media in general) for characters to act more familiar than one might have expected in a parallel real-life situation. It's what the audience expects within the fantasy of fiction media, and what allows the writers to enjoy themselves.

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Tue Jun 29, 2010 4:35 pm So what would be the "normal" or "natural" development of Shinji and Asuka's relationship for the course of four months?

[]:             Quote Post by esselfortium » Tue Jun 29, 2010 4:39 pm Presumably something that vaguely resembles the openness we see onscreen and not the anxiety that we're mostly just told about until later on in the show. Also, I see the problem here as twofold (at least): All the other things that go on don't make sense in such a short time as suggested, and it's not believable development based on the bits of it we see onscreen or especially not based on a fudged timeline that'd be long enough for the other events surrounding them to be believable.
